linked_sections.paper_title: Language-Routing Mixture of Experts for Multilingual and Code-Switching Speech Recognition
linked_sections.heading: Abstract
linked_sections.text_snippet: In this work, we propose a computation-efficient network named Language-Routing Mixture of Experts (LR-MoE) for multilingual and code-switching ASR. LR-MoE extracts language-specific representations through the Mixture of Language Experts (MLE), which is guided to learn by a frame-wise language routing mechanism. The weight-shared frame-level language identification (LID) network is jointly trained as the shared pre-router of each MoE layer. Experiments show that the proposed method significantly improves multilingual and code-switching speech recognition performances over baseline with comparable computational efficiency.
linked_sections.reason: The paper's core contribution is proposing LR-MoE with a language routing mechanism to improve multilingual ASR performance. The user's idea proposes a similar mechanism (MoE with language identity token routing) to improve multilingual LLM performance. The underlying contribution of improving multilingual performance via language-aware MoE routing is very similar, differing mainly in the specific task (ASR vs LLM).

papers.title: Multilingual Routing in Mixture-of-Experts
papers.abstract: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures have become the key to scaling modern LLMs, yet little is understood about how their sparse routing dynamics respond to multilingual data. In this work, we analyze expert routing patterns using parallel multilingual datasets and present highly interpretable layer-wise phenomena. We find that MoE models route tokens in language-specific ways in the early and late decoder layers but exhibit significant cross-lingual routing alignment in middle layers, mirroring parameter-sharing trends observed in dense LLMs. In particular, we reveal a clear, strong correlation between a model's performance in a given language and how similarly its tokens are routed to English in these layers. Extending beyond correlation, we explore inference-time interventions that induce higher cross-lingual routing alignment. We introduce a method that steers the router by promoting middle-layer task experts frequently activated in English, and it successfully increases multilingual performance. These 1-2% gains are remarkably consistent across two evaluation tasks, three models, and 15+ languages, especially given that these simple interventions override routers of extensively trained, state-of-the-art LLMs. In comparison, interventions outside of the middle layers or targeting multilingual-specialized experts only yield performance degradation. Altogether, we present numerous findings that explain how MoEs process non-English text and demonstrate that generalization is limited by the model's ability to leverage language-universal experts in all languages.

The paper, while also focused on multilingual MoE LLMs, primarily investigates existing routing patterns and then introduces an inference-time intervention method that 'steers the router by promoting middle-layer task experts frequently activated in English' to increase multilingual performance. While both address multilingual MoE performance, the paper's problem is more about understanding and intervening in existing MoE routing, rather than a novel conditioning mechanism based on detected language identity. | Method: The user's idea proposes conditioning the router on a detected language identity token, which is a direct input-based conditioning. The paper, while also focused on multilingual MoE routing, primarily analyzes existing routing patterns and then introduces an inference-time intervention method that 'steers the router by promoting middle-layer task experts frequently activated in English' rather than directly conditioning on a language identity token. Both approaches involve manipulating MoE routing for multilingual performance, but the specific mechanism of conditioning differs. | Domain: Both the user's research idea and the paper focus on improving multilingual LLM performance using Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures. The paper explicitly investigates 'Multilingual Routing in Mixture-of-Experts' and introduces 'inference-time interventions that induce higher cross-lingual routing alignment' to increase multilingual performance, directly aligning with the user's goal of improving multilingual LLM performance via routing mechanisms. | Contribution: The user's idea proposes a specific intervention: conditioning the router on a detected language identity token. The paper, while exploring multilingual routing in MoE models and demonstrating performance gains through 'steering the router by promoting middle-layer task experts frequently activated in English', does not propose or implement conditioning the router on a pre-detected language identity token. The paper's interventions are based on identified task-specific experts rather than explicit language identity tokens.

papers.title: Understanding Multilingualism in Mixture-of-Experts LLMs: Routing Mechanism, Expert Specialization, and Layerwise Steering
papers.abstract: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures have shown strong multilingual capabilities, yet the internal mechanisms underlying performance gains and cross-language differences remain insufficiently understood. In this work, we conduct a systematic analysis of MoE models, examining routing behavior and expert specialization across languages and network depth. Our analysis reveals that multilingual processing in MoE models is highly structured: routing aligns with linguistic families, expert utilization follows a clear layerwise pattern, and high-resource languages rely on shared experts while low-resource languages depend more on language-exclusive experts despite weaker performance. Layerwise interventions further show that early and late MoE layers support language-specific processing, whereas middle layers serve as language-agnostic capacity hubs. Building on these insights, we propose a routing-guided steering method that adaptively guides routing behavior in middle layers toward shared experts associated with dominant languages at inference time, leading to consistent multilingual performance improvements, particularly for linguistically related language pairs. Our code is available at https://github.com/conctsai/Multilingualism-in-Mixture-of-Experts-LLMs.
papers.reason: Problem: The user's idea proposes using language identity tokens to condition the router in a mixture-of-experts (MoE) LLM to improve multilingual performance. The paper, while also focused on multilingual MoE LLMs, primarily aims to understand the internal mechanisms of existing MoE models, examining routing behavior and expert specialization across languages and network depth. It then proposes a 'routing-guided steering method' that adaptively guides routing behavior towards shared experts at inference time, which is a different approach than conditioning the router on language identity tokens at preprocessing. | Method: The user's idea proposes conditioning the MoE router on a detected language identity token, which is a direct input to the router. The paper, while also focusing on MoE routing for multilingual LLMs, conditions the router on the hidden representation `h_l(x)` of the token, which implicitly contains language information. The paper then proposes a 'routing-guided steering method' that adaptively guides routing behavior by modifying router logits based on language association strength, which is a post-hoc intervention rather than a direct input conditioning like the user's idea. | Domain: Both the user's research idea and the paper are within the same sub-area of improving multilingual LLM performance using Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures. The paper analyzes routing behavior and expert specialization in MoE LLMs for multilingual processing and proposes a 'routing-guided steering method' to improve performance, which is closely related to the user's idea of using a 'mixture-of-experts routing mechanism' for multilingual LLMs. | Contribution: The user's idea proposes conditioning the MoE router on a detected language identity token. The paper, while analyzing multilingual MoE routing behavior and proposing a 'routing-guided steering method' to improve performance, conditions its router on the input's hidden representation (h_l(x)) as stated in Section 2, and steers based on 'normalized routing frequency' and 'original routing logit' (Section 7.1), not a pre-detected language identity token.

papers.title: Language-Routing Mixture of Experts for Multilingual and Code-Switching Speech Recognition
papers.abstract: Multilingual speech recognition for both monolingual and code-switching speech is a challenging task. Recently, based on the Mixture of Experts (MoE), many works have made good progress in multilingual and code-switching ASR, but present huge computational complexity with the increase of supported languages. In this work, we propose a computation-efficient network named Language-Routing Mixture of Experts (LR-MoE) for multilingual and code-switching ASR. LR-MoE extracts language-specific representations through the Mixture of Language Experts (MLE), which is guided to learn by a frame-wise language routing mechanism. The weight-shared frame-level language identification (LID) network is jointly trained as the shared pre-router of each MoE layer. Experiments show that the proposed method significantly improves multilingual and code-switching speech recognition performances over baseline with comparable computational efficiency.

The paper's contributions and methodology are nearly identical to the user's research idea, differing only in the application domain (ASR vs. LLM).

papers.title: MoE-LPR: Multilingual Extension of Large Language Models through Mixture-of-Experts with Language Priors Routing
papers.abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are often English-centric due to the disproportionate distribution of languages in their pre-training data. Enhancing non-English language capabilities through post-pretraining often results in catastrophic forgetting of the ability of original languages. Previous methods either achieve good expansion with severe forgetting or slight forgetting with poor expansion, indicating the challenge of balancing language expansion while preventing forgetting. In this paper, we propose a method called MoE-LPR (Mixture-of-Experts with Language Priors Routing) to alleviate this problem. MoE-LPR employs a two-stage training approach to enhance the multilingual capability. First, the model is post-pretrained into a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture by upcycling, where all the original parameters are frozen and new experts are added. In this stage, we focus improving the ability on expanded languages, without using any original language data. Then, the model reviews the knowledge of the original languages with replay data amounting to less than 1% of post-pretraining, where we incorporate language priors routing to better recover the abilities of the original languages. Evaluations on multiple benchmarks show that MoE-LPR outperforms other post-pretraining methods. Freezing original parameters preserves original language knowledge while adding new experts preserves the learning ability. Reviewing with LPR enables effective utilization of multilingual knowledge within the parameters. Additionally, the MoE architecture maintains the same inference overhead while increasing total model parameters. Extensive experiments demonstrate MoE-LPR's effectiveness in improving expanded languages and preserving original language proficiency with superior scalability. Code and scripts are freely available at https://github.com/zjwang21/MoE-LPR.git.

papers.title: Less, but Better: Efficient Multilingual Expansion for LLMs via Layer-wise Mixture-of-Experts
papers.abstract: Continually expanding new languages for existing large language models (LLMs) is a promising yet challenging approach to building powerful multilingual LLMs. The biggest challenge is to make the model continuously learn new languages while preserving the proficient ability of old languages. To achieve this, recent work utilizes the Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture to expand new languages by adding new experts and avoid catastrophic forgetting of old languages by routing corresponding tokens to the original model backbone (old experts). Although intuitive, this kind of method is parameter-costly when expanding new languages and still inevitably impacts the performance of old languages. To address these limitations, we analyze the language characteristics of different layers in LLMs and propose a layer-wise expert allocation algorithm (LayerMoE) to determine the appropriate number of new experts for each layer. Specifically, we find different layers in LLMs exhibit different representation similarities between languages and then utilize the similarity as the indicator to allocate experts for each layer, i.e., the higher similarity, the fewer experts. Additionally, to further mitigate the forgetting of old languages, we add a classifier in front of the router network on the layers with higher similarity to guide the routing of old language tokens. Experimental results show that our method outperforms the previous state-of-the-art baseline with 60% fewer experts in the single-expansion setting and with 33.3% fewer experts in the lifelong-expansion setting, demonstrating the effectiveness of our method.

Both address multilingual LLM performance with MoE routing, but the specific mechanism for conditioning the router differs. | Method: Both the user's idea and the paper propose using a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture for multilingual LLMs. The paper's 'Routing Classification Network' explicitly adds a classifier in front of the router to guide routing based on language, which aligns with the user's idea of conditioning the router on language identity. However, the paper's primary novelty, 'Layer-wise Expert Allocation' based on hidden state similarity, and its use of language priors routing (LPR) are distinct from the user's specific proposal of using a pre-detected language identity token directly. | Domain: Both the user's research idea and the paper focus on improving multilingual LLM performance. The paper states, 'we add a classifier in front of the router network on the layers with higher similarity to guide the routing of old language tokens' and 'During inference, the tokens classified to old languages are directly routed to the old expert, while others classified to new languages are normally routed following Eq.(3)'. While the user's idea specifies 'detected at preprocessing' and the paper's classifier is 'in front of the router network', the core mechanism of using language identity to guide routing is largely overlapping.

existing_examples.name: Multilingual Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) Models
existing_examples.description: Many MoE models for multilingual tasks already exist. While the *exact* conditioning mechanism (language identity token vs. embedding) might vary, the core idea of using language-specific routing in MoE for multilingual LLMs is a well-established research area.

existing_examples.name: Language-specific routing in MoE
existing_examples.description: Research papers and implementations (e.g., some variations of GLaM, Switch Transformers, or other MoE architectures adapted for multilingualism) have explored various ways to route inputs based on language. This often involves either explicit language ID or implicit language features learned from embeddings.

existing_examples.name: Explicit language ID in multilingual models
existing_examples.description: It's common practice in multilingual NLP to use explicit language ID tokens or embeddings as input features to guide the model's behavior, even outside of MoE architectures. Applying this to MoE routing is a natural extension.

reality_check.assessment: The core idea of using a mixture-of-experts (MoE) architecture for multilingual LLMs and routing based on language identity is not novel. Various research efforts have explored multilingual MoE models, and the use of explicit language identity tokens to guide model behavior (including routing) is a known technique in multilingual NLP. While the specific implementation detail of conditioning the router *directly* on a pre-detected language identity token rather than the input embedding might be a slight variation, it falls within the scope of existing research on multilingual MoE and language-aware routing.
novelty_aspects: The explicit comparison and potential demonstration of superior performance when conditioning the router *solely* on a pre-detected language identity token compared to conditioning on the input embedding itself, especially if it leads to significant efficiency or performance gains not yet widely reported.
A novel method for robust and efficient language identity detection specifically optimized for this routing mechanism.
A new architecture for the router itself that leverages this language identity token in a unique way.
reality_check.recommendation: While the overarching concept exists, there might be novelty in demonstrating a significant advantage of your specific routing mechanism (language ID token vs. input embedding) through rigorous experimentation. Focus on quantifying the benefits (e.g., performance, efficiency, interpretability) and comparing it against state-of-the-art multilingual MoE models that use different routing strategies. Clearly articulate *why* conditioning on the language identity token is superior in your specific setup.

matched_sections.paper_title: Language-Routing Mixture of Experts for Multilingual and Code-Switching Speech Recognition
matched_sections.heading: Abstract
matched_sections.text_snippet: In this work, we propose a computation-efficient network named Language-Routing Mixture of Experts (LR-MoE) for multilingual and code-switching ASR. LR-MoE extracts language-specific representations through the Mixture of Language Experts (MLE), which is guided to learn by a frame-wise language routing mechanism. The weight-shared frame-level language identification (LID) network is jointly trained as the shared pre-router of each MoE layer.

matched_sections.heading: Abstract

matched_sections.paper_title: Less, but Better: Efficient Multilingual Expansion for LLMs via Layer-wise Mixture-of-Experts
matched_sections.heading: Abstract
matched_sections.text_snippet: Continually expanding new languages for existing large language models (LLMs) is a promising yet challenging approach to building powerful multilingual LLMs. The biggest challenge is to make the model continuously learn new languages while preserving the proficient ability of old languages. To achieve this, recent work utilizes the Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture to expand new languages by adding new experts and avoid catastrophic forgetting of old languages by routing corresponding tokens to the original model backbone (old experts).
matched_sections.reason: Both the user's idea and the paper aim to improve multilingual LLM performance, specifically addressing challenges related to expanding new languages and avoiding catastrophic forgetting in existing LLMs using MoE architecture. The user's idea proposes a specific mechanism within this broader problem.

matched_sections.reason: The user's idea proposes using a mixture-of-experts routing mechanism to improve multilingual LLM performance. The paper also utilizes MoE architecture for multilingual expansion and addresses routing, showing a shared high-level contribution.

matched_sections.paper_title: Less, but Better: Efficient Multilingual Expansion for LLMs via Layer-wise Mixture-of-Experts
matched_sections.heading: Conclusion
matched_sections.text_snippet: Additionally, we add a classifier in front of the router network for the layers with higher similarity to further mitigate the forgetting of old languages. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of our method, which is parameter-efficient and achieves good expansion of new languages and less forgetting of old languages.
matched_sections.reason: The user's idea aims to improve multilingual LLM performance using a specific routing mechanism. The paper's conclusion highlights its method's effectiveness in achieving good expansion of new languages and less forgetting of old languages, which aligns with the user's goal of improving multilingual LLM performance.

sentence_annotations.sentence: Using a mixture-of-experts routing mechanism where the router is conditioned on the input's language identity token (detected at preprocessing) rather than the input embedding, to improve multilingual LLM performance.

linked_sections.paper_title: MoE-LPR: Multilingual Extension of Large Language Models through Mixture-of-Experts with Language Priors Routing
linked_sections.heading: Abstract
linked_sections.text_snippet: In this paper, we propose a method called MoE-LPR (Mixture-of-Experts with Language Priors Routing) to alleviate this problem. MoE-LPR employs a two-stage training approach to enhance the multilingual capability.
linked_sections.reason: Both the user's idea and the paper aim to improve multilingual LLM performance, specifically addressing challenges in enhancing non-English language capabilities.

linked_sections.paper_title: MoE-LPR: Multilingual Extension of Large Language Models through Mixture-of-Experts with Language Priors Routing
linked_sections.heading: Abstract
linked_sections.text_snippet: In this paper, we propose a method called MoE-LPR (Mixture-of-Experts with Language Priors Routing) to alleviate this problem. MoE-LPR employs a two-stage training approach to enhance the multilingual capability.
linked_sections.reason: The user's idea proposes 'Using a mixture-of-experts routing mechanism' which is the core of the paper's 'MoE-LPR (Mixture-of-Experts with Language Priors Routing)' method.

linked_sections.heading: ABSTRACT
linked_sections.text_snippet: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures have become the key to scaling modern LLMs, yet little is understood about how their sparse routing dynamics respond to multilingual data.
linked_sections.reason: Both the user's idea and the paper are focused on Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures and their application to multilingual Large Language Models (LLMs).

linked_sections.paper_title: Language-Routing Mixture of Experts for Multilingual and Code-Switching Speech Recognition
linked_sections.heading: Abstract
linked_sections.text_snippet: In this work, we propose a computation-efficient network named Language-Routing Mixture of Experts (LR-MoE) for multilingual and code-switching ASR. LR-MoE extracts language-specific representations through the Mixture of Language Experts (MLE), which is guided to learn by a frame-wise language routing mechanism. The weight-shared frame-level language identification (LID) network is jointly trained as the shared pre-router of each MoE layer. Experiments show that the proposed method significantly improves multilingual and code-switching speech recognition performances over baseline with comparable computational efficiency.
linked_sections.reason: The paper's core contribution is proposing LR-MoE with a language routing mechanism to improve multilingual ASR performance. The user's idea proposes a similar mechanism (MoE with language identity token routing) to improve multilingual LLM performance. The underlying contribution of improving multilingual performance via language-aware MoE routing is very similar, differing mainly in the specific task (ASR vs LLM).
